coredata.dc:title: Investigations on paraffin wax/CQD composite phase change material - Improved latent heat and thermal stability
coredata.dc:creator: Emeema, J.
Murali, G.
Reddi, B. Venkateswara
Mangesh, V.L.
coredata.dc:description: The growing disparity between energy demand and supply has rendered the storage of thermal energy essential. In this study, experiments have been conducted on novel composite Phase Change Materials (PCMs) comprising Paraffin Wax (PW) as base PCM dispersed with 1 %, 5 %, 10 %, 15 %, and 20 % weights of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) to investigate their latent heat and thermal stability. These studies are crucial to determine the energy storage capacity of the composite PCMs. Latent heat and melting temperatures were determined using a Differential Scanning Calorimeter (DSC). The latent heat of all composite PCMs increased when compared with PW with a maximum enhancement of 65.1 % for 1 wt% composite PCM. The melting temperature range of composites is higher than PW. Thermogravimetric analysis showed an improved thermal stability of all composite PCMs than PW. These results unequivocally suggest the employability of these PCMs for thermal energy storage in the medium temperature range.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Paraffin wax
Carbon quantum dots
Phase change material
Latent heat
Melting temperature
Thermal stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological images of Composite PCMs captured by SEM.
DSC curves for PW and Composite PCMs.
Melting temperature curves for PW and Composite PCMs.
Degradation of weight with temperatures for PW and composite PCMs.
DSC curves for PW after 0, 100, 200 and 300 thermal cycles.
DSC curves for PWCQD-1 after 0, 100, 200 and 300 thermal cycles.
TGA curves for PW after 0, 100, 200 and 300 thermal cycles.
TGA curves for PWCQD-1 after 0, 100, 200 and 300 thermal cycles.
Literature review - PW and carbon composites.

row.entry: Latent heat increased by 18.8 %, 21.4 %, and 44.4 % respectively.

a. Paraffin/Expanded Graphite (EG)
b. Paraffin/ Graphene oxide (GO)
c. Paraffin/Graphene (GR)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Latent Heat values of PW and Composite PCMs.
Onset degradation temperatures of PW and composite PCMs.
Latent heat values of PW and PWCQD-1 after 0, 100, 200 and 300 cycles.
Melting temperatures of PW and PWCQD-1 after 0, 100, 200 and 300 cycles.
Onset degradation temperatures of PW and PWCQD-1 after 0,100, 200 and 300 cycles.

head.ce:title: Investigations on paraffin wax/CQD composite phase change material - Improved latent heat and thermal stability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation, Vaddeswaram, Guntur District, 522502, Andhra Pradesh, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation, Vaddeswaram, Guntur District-522502, Andhra Pradesh, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Vidya Jyothi Institute of Technology, Hyderabad, Telangana 500075, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Vidya Jyothi Institute of Technology, Hyderabad, Telangana-500075 India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The growing disparity between energy demand and supply has rendered the storage of thermal energy essential. In this study, experiments have been conducted on novel composite Phase Change Materials (PCMs) comprising Paraffin Wax (PW) as base PCM dispersed with 1 %, 5 %, 10 %, 15 %, and 20 % weights of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) to investigate their latent heat and thermal stability. These studies are crucial to determine the energy storage capacity of the composite PCMs. Latent heat and melting temperatures were determined using a Differential Scanning Calorimeter (DSC). The latent heat of all composite PCMs increased when compared with PW with a maximum enhancement of 65.1 % for 1 wt% composite PCM. The melting temperature range of composites is higher than PW. Thermogravimetric analysis showed an improved thermal stability of all composite PCMs than PW. These results unequivocally suggest the employability of these PCMs for thermal energy storage in the medium temperature range.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy shortage is a critical challenge our world is facing, primarily because approximately 850 million persons are still without access to electricity. Forecasts indicate a potential 20–30 % surge in global energy demand from the current period to 2040, driven by an escalating need for energy-related services and improvements in energy efficiency [1]. But the use of coal and oil-based (fossil) fuels for the generation of power causes the emission of greenhouse gases and these poisonous gases cause global warming leading to significant and lasting environmental consequences. [2]. Therefore, engineers and scientists globally are driven to address these complex challenges. They are working towards creating novel technologies that reduce dependence on fossil fuels and enhance energy efficiency, but at the same time, mitigate environmental risks, reduce costs of power generation, and establish additional power plants [3]. Extensive research has been undertaken to harness dependable, cost-effective, and sustainable thermal energy sources, such as solar thermal, geothermal, and ocean thermal resources. However, a significant challenge lies in the intermittent nature of these renewable energy sources. To address this issue and to ensure the continuous supply of energy from these sources, researchers have focused on the implementation of thermal energy storage (TES) systems connecting the supply of energy and demand segments [4]. Thermal energy has the potential to be stored as a change in internal energy within a material, either through sensible heat, latent heat, thermochemical heat, or a combination of these processes. Among these processes, thermal energy storage as latent heat is particularly appealing due to its ability to provide high energy storage density.

Additionally, it possesses the unique attributes of storing heat at a constant temperature that corresponds to the phase change temperature of the material [5]. Storage of latent heat can be achieved through various phase transformations, including solid-liquid, liquid-gas, solid-gas, and solid-solid. However, for practical purposes, the two most relevant transformations are solid-liquid and solid-solid [6]. The solid-liquid transition has demonstrated economic attractiveness for utilization in TES systems [5]. There are different approaches to storing latent energy, but the method of employing phase change materials (PCMs) stands out for their exceptional ability [7–11]. The amount of energy stored during a phase change is dependent on PCM properties. Based on their material properties, PCMs can be categorized primarily into organic and inorganic types [12]. The organic category comprises paraffin and fatty acids, while the inorganic category encompasses salt hydrates and metallic substances [13,14].

There has been significant research on organic PCMs, aiming to develop high-performance composite PCMs capable of accomplishing many functions that can be utilized for thermal energy management systems [15]. Among the organic PCMs, Paraffin Wax (PW) exhibits the most versatile range of melting temperatures. It possesses a high latent heat and favorable thermal characteristics, including minimal supercooling. The cost-effectiveness, chemical stability, and non-toxicity of this material make it a perfect choice for engineering applications such as solar thermal energy storage, recovery of industrial waste heat, and serving as insulation material for buildings. PW is a popular PCM for use in thermal energy storage systems that store latent heat [16–18]. Despite PW possessing high latent heat, its low thermal conductivity hinders the heat transfer rate, resulting in slower charging and discharging. Additionally, the amorphous nature of paraffin in its liquid phase restricts its engineering applications. To address these challenges, numerous studies have been undertaken and are found in the existing literature. One recently recommended method to enhance the thermal conductivity of PW involves incorporating nanoparticles with high conductivity into the PCM to prepare composite phase change materials (CPCMs) [18]. The research community is driven to enhance various factors in PCMs such as thermal conductivity, latent heat, specific heat, thermal stability, thermal reliability, photothermal performance, and more [16].

CPCMs find applications in various fields owing to their ability to store and release thermal energy effectively. Numerous reviews in the field of PCM applications in buildings, spanning from 2003 to the present, highlight the critical importance of energy storage in building systems [19–21]. The primary objective is the incorporation of PCMs into walls, ceilings, and floors of the buildings to regulate indoor temperatures, to improve energy efficiency in buildings, to reduce heating and cooling costs and to create more comfortable living or working environments. PCMs are used in energy storage for storing excess energy during off-peak times for later use during peak demand with the main purpose of balancing energy consumption, reducing reliance on peak power generation, and enhancing the efficiency of renewable energy sources such as solar energy [22–26]. PCMs are utilized in electronic devices to manage heat generated during operation with an aim is to prevent overheating, enhance the reliability and performance of electronic components, and extend the lifespan of electronic devices. [27,28].

Murali et al. [29] reviewed different PCMs and evaluated various methods. They have concluded that carbon-based additive particles exhibit superior thermal conductivity when compared to metal-based additives. Carbon-based nanomaterials are gaining prominence as emerging materials due to their extraordinary chemical and physical properties, including superior thermal and electrical conductivity, remarkable mechanical strength, and versatile optical attributes. These qualities broaden their utility in various applications, ranging from biosensors to energy conversion and storage devices [30].

Xie et al. [31] conducted experiments on Paraffin wax with different concentrations of paraffin/high-density polyethylene (HDPE) and expandable graphite (EG) combined. They found that for 2 wt% HDPE and 0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 weight percentages of EG, the latent heat was reduced by about 25 %. Haghighi et al. [32] added CuO, TiO2, Al2O3, and graphene to paraffin in the mass fractions of 1 %, 2 %, and 3 %. The findings demonstrated that introducing nanoparticles into paraffin reduced composites' onset melting temperature and latent heat. Araujo. et al. [33] performed experiments with 1, 5, and 10 wt% of graphene nanofillers and paraffin composite PCMs. They found that the latent heat and thermal stability of composites improved as mass fractions of nano-graphene increased. The study revealed that adding 10 % graphene resulted in a remarkable 40 % increase in thermal storage capacity.

Murali et al. [34] performed an experiment using paraffin and graphite powder composite phase change material (CPCM) on a Trapezoidal Battery Pack with a liquid cooling system. They observed that thermal conductivity increased from 0.25 to 2.7 W/mK and heat transfer rate increased significantly. Usman et al. [35] conducted experiments on PW and graphite PCM to improve the thermal conductivity of PW. For 2, 4, and 6 weight percentages of graphite, they obtained an outstanding 170 %, 190 %, and 200 % enhancement in thermal conductivities respectively. Considerable research has been conducted in the realm of enhancing the thermophysical properties of PCMs with carbon materials for their application in thermal energy storage.

Xu et al. [36] in 2004 stumbled upon a novel carbon material during their efforts to generate single-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs). This newly identified substance was termed carbon nanoparticles (CNPs). Subsequently, in 2006, Sun et al. [37] instituted a laser ablation technique to produce Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) with markedly enhanced fluorescence emission. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a promising substitute for semiconductor quantum dots possessing exceptional attributes and advantages. They are less toxic, biocompatible, affordable, and possess chemical inertness. They exhibit comparable fluorescence properties, rendering them an appealing option for a range of applications [38]. Beyond these qualities, CQDs are distinguished by outstanding optoelectronic properties. They can be synthesized by simple methods and have considerable specific surface area. These characteristics of CQDs have ignited substantial interest in research [39]. The remarkable characteristics of CQDs position them as a highly sought-after material with diverse applications across various fields.

The groundbreaking feature of this innovative research involves the development of distinctive composite PCMs by integrating CQDs into PW. Based on the existing literature, there is a notable scarcity of research thus far on the incorporation of CQDs into PW to create composite phase change materials, with a specific focus on exploring their thermal properties for application in thermal energy storage. Carbon dots, renowned for their superior thermal conductivity and light-absorbing capabilities, enhance the overall thermal performance of the composite. Their ability to efficiently absorb and dissipate heat contributes to improved heat transfer within the material, ensuring rapid and effective thermal energy storage. PW with high latent heat capacity complements the carbon dots by providing a reliable means of storing and releasing thermal energy. The phase transition of PW allows for the absorption or release of energy during the melting or solidification process, making it a highly effective medium for thermal energy storage applications. The combination of these materials creates a composite PCM with advantages of dual functionality that excels in both thermal conductivity and latent heat capacity. This harmonious effect not only enhances the overall efficiency of the material but also opens new possibilities for exploration in various thermal energy storage applications.

The cost of synthesizing or procuring carbon dots plays a crucial role in obtaining cost-effective composite phase change material. Depending on the synthesis methods, raw material costs, and scalability of production, the overall cost of the composite material can vary. Researchers are continually exploring ways to enhance the green synthesis of CQDs and optimize their environmental performance. Deriving carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from waste sources such as plant materials, particularly fruits and vegetables, biomass which is renewable presents the advantage of a basic, cost-effective, environmentally friendly, and readily available processes in nature [39,40]. In recent years, pyrolysis has emerged as a potent technique for producing fluorescent carbon dots, utilizing macroscopic carbon structures as precursors. This method boasts advantages such as a brief reaction time, cost-effectiveness, ease of operation, solvent-free processes, and scalable production [41]. The cost advantage may vary depending on the specific application. The overall market dynamics, including supply and demand for PCMs and carbon dots, can influence costs.

The prelude for investigations on these composite PCMs for thermal energy storage is to study the values of thermal conductivity and latent heat of the composite, as these properties play a pivotal role in the applications of energy storage. Thermal conductivity facilitates rapid heat transfer inside the PCM, and it also aids in a faster rate of heat transfer from the source material enabling efficient heat exchange. Latent heat storage quantifies the amount of energy the PCM can store before a significant temperature increase takes place. [16]. Latent heat is a crucial characteristic of PCMs, as it determines their effectiveness in thermal energy storage applications. This property makes PCMs valuable in applications like energy-efficient building materials, temperature control in electronics, and solar energy storage.
Morphological and thermal conductivity investigations on novel composite PCMs, PW/CQD composite PCMs have been conducted by the authors and the results have been presented [42].

Table 1 provides a brief literature review of the latent heat results of the composite PCMs made of PW and carbon materials. It is clear that there is no systematic pattern in the results, and latent heat decreased in most cases with the addition of carbon particles.

In this study, the crucial factors in the storage of thermal energy like latent heat, melting temperatures, and thermal stability of these composite PCMs have been investigated with the help of DSC and TGA analysis. Thermal reliability of these composites has been studied by conducting a thermal cycling test for 0, 100, 200 and 300 cycles. CQDs were added to PW in the weight percentages of 1, 5, 10, 15, and 20. These weight percentages have been considered based on a literature review on various carbon materials added to PW with an intention to encompass low and high weight percentages. These investigations provide invaluable tools for evaluating the practicality and adaptability of these composite PCMs in a wide range of temperature applications for thermal energy storage.

PW with a melting temperature range between 60 °C to 64 °C was purchased from Krokio Products Pvt. Ltd. Mumbai India. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) powder was procured from Metro Composites Laboratory, Chennai, India.

The preparation process involved initially melting the PW at approximately 70 °C. Subsequently, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) of 1 %, 5 %, 10 %,15 %, and 20 % by weight were added to the molten wax in the specified proportions. To ensure the homogeneous distribution of CQDs, this mixture underwent a 30-min ultrasonication process in the ultrasonicator, that could be operated at 40 kHz and 120 W during which it was simultaneously heated and subjected to vibration. This method effectively prevented the aggregation of nanoparticles and maintained their stability within the mixture. The resulting finely blended composition which was in a molten state was then transferred to silicon molds and allowed to cool until it solidified at room temperature. Subsequently, the prepared samples underwent a thorough visual examination to identify and rectify any surface defects before being sent for further experimentation. A visual representation of the sample preparation procedure is provided in Fig. 1 .
The prepared composite PCM samples were labeled according to their weight percentages added, as shown in Table 2 .

Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) is the most employed thermal analysis method to quantify the energy absorbed or released by a sample during heating or cooling. In a standard DSC analysis, a minute sample (1–15 mg) is carefully positioned inside a sealed crucible. An empty crucible functions as a reference to meticulously calibrate the system. The testing procedure involves subjecting the sample to controlled heating or cooling at a specified rate, conducted under a designated purge gas with a controlled flow rate. Simultaneously, a suitable sensing device continually monitors the disparity in heat input between the reference and the sample, which arises from energy changes within the sample. DSC gauges temperatures of both a reference material and a sample by varying the sample temperature according to a programmed sequence. It subsequently quantifies the heat generated from the temperature disparity between the two. The ensuing heat flow into the sample is diligently monitored and analyzed to discern and characterize phase transitions and reactions.

The DSC analysis in this study was carried out employing a Shimadzu Differential Scanning Calorimeter (Model: DSC-60 A). The temperature range of this instrument is −140 °C to 600 °C. A 2 mg sample was positioned within an aluminum pan during the testing procedure. The test environment was maintained under an inert gas (nitrogen) atmosphere to prevent the oxidation of the sample. The test conditions were diligently configured within a temperature range spanning from 30 °C to 120 °C. The test was done with a controlled heating rate of 10 °C per minute to ensure proper testing and accuracy of the results.

The DSC measurement is characterized by high accuracy, within ±3 % across the temperature range, and demonstrates notable reproducibility. Typical temperature accuracy of DSC instrument is ±0.2 K and temperature precision is ±0.02 K. Variations in room temperature can lead to drift in DSC data. Every measuring instrument, regardless of its precision and accuracy, inherently carries a level of uncertainty. This arises from two primary factors: the constraints inherent in the measuring instrument, which introduce systematic errors, and the proficiency of the experimenter conducting the measurements, contributing to random errors. Generally, instruments with greater precision exhibit lower levels of uncertainty.

Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) is a material analysis method where the weight of a sample is assessed as a function of temperature or time as the sample undergoes controlled heating. TGA helps to determine the temperatures corresponding to the onset and maximum rate of weight loss, establish the upper-temperature limits of polymers to assist the manufacturers in anticipating and addressing potential performance issues under extreme environmental conditions. TGA is also capable of quantifying moisture and volatile contents in a given sample.

A Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) setup comprises a sample placed in a pan and enclosed in a furnace which is supported by a precise balance. The pan undergoes heating or cooling throughout the experiment, with the mass of the sample continuously monitored. The sample environment is regulated by a purge gas, which can be either inert or reactive, flowing over the sample and exiting through an exhaust.

TGA - DTG 60 SHIMADZU was employed as a fundamental instrument to evaluate the thermal stability of the composite materials under study. The temperature range of this instrument is ambient to 1100 °C. In this experiment, a precise 2 mg sample was carefully situated within an alumina crucible, and the continuous monitoring of weight variation took place over a temperature spectrum spanning from 30 °C to 600 °C. Approximately the same sample weight must be used during each experiment to ensure reproducibility. To ensure the prevention of oxidation of the sample and the reliability of the results, a controlled nitrogen gas atmosphere was meticulously maintained during the entire test, and a consistent heating rate of 10 °C per minute was strictly adhered to. TGA method allows for the detection of weight changes with remarkable precision, often accurate to a fraction of a microgram. Typical temperature precision of TGA instrument is ±0.8 °C.

The morphology and microstructures of composite PCMs captured by HITACHI S-3700 N Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) for magnifications of 750×, 1000×, and 1500× have been presented in Figs. 2 to 6. The images clearly demonstrate the effective dispersion of CQDs within the Polymeric matrix integrated seamlessly into the layers of PW, showcasing their presence, and beautifully highlighting the fluorescent characteristics of CQDs [41].

The assessment of the PCMs in terms of their energy storage capabilities is made by the latent heat values obtained from the findings of the DSC investigation. The latent heat curves of PW and composite PCMs obtained from DSC investigation for different weight percentages of CQDs are displayed in Fig. 7 .

The initial minor peak is attributed to a solid-to-solid phase transition within the phase peak, whereas the subsequent prominent peak is a result of the transition from solid to liquid state. The solid-to-solid phase change exhibits a relatively low enthalpy change during the phase transition, and typically, there are minor alterations in volume compared to transitions between solid and liquid phases [46]. The transformation from a solid state to another solid-state involves the reorganization of the molecular structure, shifting it from a state of ordered arrangement to one of disorderly configuration as attributed by many researchers [47]. In this study, PW and composites undergo a transition between solid states within the temperature range of 43 °C to 59 °C. The phase transition begins at 43 °C and concludes within 71 °C - 74 °C. The cumulative area beneath both phase transition peaks is utilized for the determination of the overall latent heat of PW and its composites [44].
Latent heat values for PW and composite PCMs obtained by the DSC test are tabulated as shown in Table 3 along with their enhancement ratio percentages.

It is worth emphasizing that composite PCMs exhibit considerably higher latent heat values compared to pure PW. This observation distinctly underscores the exceptional energy storage capacity of composite PCMs in comparison to PW.

The highest latent heat value was attained at PWCQD-1, exhibiting an enhancement ratio of 65.1 %. The latent heat values gradually decreased as the weight percentages of carbon dots increased. Studies have suggested that the increase in latent heat of composite PCMs can be attributed to the intermolecular interaction or bond between the PW and molecules of nanoparticles added. The large surface area of the nanoparticles may also lead to intermolecular interaction between PW and nanoparticles [48]. This intermolecular interaction between PW and nanoparticles may be due to Vander Waals forces [49]. The formation of intermolecular bonds takes the place of some of the PW PCM mass that contributes to latent heat. The resulting change in latent heat capacity depends on whether the strength of the intermolecular bond or the extent of mass replacement has a greater impact. If the intermolecular bond is stronger than the mass replacement, the composite PCM requires more energy to undergo a phase change from solid to liquid, resulting in a higher latent heat capacity. Conversely, if the mass replacement is stronger than the intermolecular bond, the latent heat capacity of the composite PCM decreases. At lower concentrations, the intermolecular bond is more significant than the mass replacement, leading to an increase in latent heat capacity. However, at higher concentrations, the mass of PCM replaced becomes dominant, leading to a decrease in latent heat capacity [12,50]. The substantial loading of nanoparticles makes it unfeasible for the molecules within the PCM matrix to reconfigure, contributing significantly to the reduction in the effective latent heat of the composite [51]. The interplay between intermolecular forces and mass replacement in the composite PCM system can significantly impact its latent heat capacity and may have important implications for various thermal energy storage applications.

The remarkably higher latent heat exhibited by composite Phase Change Materials (PCMs) in comparison to pure PW underlines their outstanding energy storage capacity, rendering them exceptionally well-suited for a diverse array of thermal energy storage applications.

The melting temperature of PCMs is the temperature at which these materials change their phase from a solid to a liquid. It is a crucial property of PCMs, as it determines the specific temperature range at which they can effectively absorb and release thermal energy. PCMs are selected based on their melting temperature to match the requirements of various applications. The choice of a PCM with a specific melting temperature depends on the intended application, such as passive building temperature control, electronics cooling, or solar energy storage.

The melting temperatures of PCMs are determined from the onset temperatures of the DSC curves [52]. The melting temperature details of PW and composite PCMs are presented in Table 4 . Fig. 8 shows the melting temperature variations for different weight percentages of CQDs.

Phase change for PW starts at 63.1 °C and ends at 71.8 °C. Melting temperatures increased with respect to PW, for PWCQD-1 and PWCQD-5 but decreased for the remaining composites. Investigators discovered that the interactions among the composite components significantly influence the directional shift of melting temperature. In the case of a mildly attractive interaction between the fluid and the matrix surface, a lowered melting phase transition temperature will manifest [53]. On the contrary, with a strongly attractive interaction between the fluid and the matrix surface, an elevated melting phase transition temperature will manifest.

It can be noted that the phase transition of all composites ends at a higher temperature than that of PW. The melting temperature range of composites is larger than PW. The temperature range at which phase change occurs is influenced by the physical and chemical characteristics of the phase change material utilized. The material's thermophysical characteristics can impact the melting and solidification duration. The introduction of nanoparticles alters these thermophysical properties, thereby influencing the melting duration [54].

Thermogravimetric Analyzer (TGA) is utilized to assess the thermal stability of the produced composites. PCMs are commonly used in applications where they need to repeatedly store and release thermal energy. Ensuring their thermal stability guarantees consistent and reliable performance over numerous thermal cycles. A thermally stable PCM is more likely to have a longer operational life span, reducing the need for frequent replacements or maintenance. Knowing the temperature range at which a PCM remains stable helps in designing and optimizing systems for efficient energy storage and release. The thermal stability of PCMs is thus critical for their successful integration into various applications, ensuring safety, reliability, and efficiency in thermal energy storage systems.

Fig. 9 illustrates the decrease in weight percent of the composite PCMs due to temperature changes. The initial degradation temperature was identified when a 3 % reduction in weight was observed in both pure paraffin and its composite PCMs [16]. In all samples, the TGA thermograms exhibited a singular prominent degradation step. This indicates that when subjecting the different samples to TGA, the resulting thermograms indicated that each sample experienced a distinct and prominent degradation event, with only one significant step in the process. This suggests that the samples underwent thermal decomposition or weight loss in a uniform and well-defined manner.
The onset degradation temperature of PW and composites is listed in Table 5 .

It is apparent that the onset degradation temperatures of all composite PCMs are greater than that of PW, increasing linearly with the percentage of CQD powder in PW. This signifies the enhanced thermal stability of all composites compared to PW. It shows that the addition of CQDs to PW possesses the ability to extend the thermal stability of the PW [16]. This also implies that the resistance to decomposition of composites is higher than PW and they can be safely used for energy storage below their degradation temperature. The thermal stability of a material mainly depends on the structure of the material and the bonds between the atoms that hold the structure together. The increase in onset degradation temperature of composites compared to PW may be attributed to the adsorption capacity of interconnected pores found in CQDs on PW. This adsorption is facilitated by a combination of capillary forces and intermolecular forces [55]. This may also be because of the presence of nanoparticles that lead to the formation of a thermal barrier that delays the breakdown of polymer chains into monomers, ultimately increasing thermal stability [56].

Evaluation of the TG test yields a total polymer content of 99.71 % for PW and the ash content left was a meager 0.29 %. For composite PCMs, the polymer content after decomposition was 99.3 %, 96.08 %, 93.87 %, 88.33 %, and 80.05 % respectively. The charred residue left was 0.7 %, 3.92 %, 6.13 %, 11.67 % and 19.95 % respectively. The results explicitly establish the high suitability of CQD material for enhancing thermal stability of PW.

A significant challenge associated with PCMs is their deterioration during repeated melt or freeze cycles. This continuous cycling often leads to a reduction in the PCM's heat storage capacity, particularly with varying melting temperatures. It is crucial to examine the developed PCM for potential degradation through a cycle stability test. Ensuring the long-term performance of PCM is vital, as cycle testing provides assurance of cost-effectiveness and time savings. The critical thermophysical properties that require consideration are the melting temperature and latent heat of fusion. [57]. For a comprehensive year-long analysis, it is recommended to conduct a minimum of 300 melt or freeze cycles. This ensures a thorough assessment, especially when a PCM experiences a complete melt and freeze cycle each day [58].

In this study, the composite PCM PWCQD-1 was chosen for the thermal cycling test owing to its superior latent heat capacity [16]. Thermal reliability of both PW and its composite PCM is assessed using a specially designed thermal test chamber. The range of temperature of this thermal test chamber is −50 °C to +150 °C. The cooling/heating rate used is 5 °C/min. The thermal cycling test is conducted within a temperature range of 30 °C to 55 °C. The data corresponding to DSC and TGA is collected for 0, 100, 200 and 300 cycles.

The DSC curves of PW and PWCQD-1 composite at 0, 100, 200 and 300 thermal cycles are shown in Figs. 10 and 11
.
The values of latent heat of fusion and melting temperatures of PW and PWCQD-1 composite are tabulated in Table 6 and Table 7 .

The latent heat increased by 3.32 % for PW at 100 cycles. This is because of the absence of the solid-solid transition region which may be accredited to the reorganization of molecules within the matrix of PW. But at 200 and 300 cycles, the latent heat for PW decreased by 0.8 % and 2.14 % respectively which may be due to performance loss caused by thermal cycling [16]. The latent heat for PWCQD-1 composite increased by 2.17 % and 0.45 % at 100 and 200 cycles and decreased slightly by 0.6 % at 300 cycles. The agglomeration of particles might be responsible for the decrease in latent heat arising from the repetitive phase change process [16]. The melting temperatures of PWCQD-1 composite PCM decreased compared to PW as can be observed from Table 7.

Thermal degradation of PW and composite PCM at 0, 100, 200 and 300 cycles can be appreciated from TGA analysis. The TGA curves of PW and PWCQD-1 composite are displayed in Figs. 12 and 13
.
The onset degradation temperatures of PW and PWCQD-1 are listed in Table 8 .

The onset of thermal degradation for PW increased by 1.48 % for 100 cycles and decreased slightly by 0.7 % and 1.86 % for 200 and 300 cycles. Thermal degradation consistently occurs between 250 °C and 400 °C. The PWCQD-1 composite PCM showed an increase of 3.98 %, 2.17 % and 1.08 % after 100, 200 and 300 cycles which highlights the commendable thermal stability of this composite PCM. The enhanced thermal stability can be attributed to the rearrangement of CQD particles following repeated thermal cycling while still preserving the thermal barrier [16].

After 0, 100, 200 and 300 cycles of thermal decomposition, the polymer content of PW was 99.91 %, 99.89 %, 99.93 % and 99.86 % and the charred residue left was 0.09 %, 0.11 %, 0.07 % and 0.14 % respectively. The polymer content of PWCQD-1 composite PCM was 98.71 %, 98.68 %, 98.69 % and 98.67 % and the charred residue left was 1.29 %, 1.32 %, 1.31 % and 1.33 % respectively for 0, 100, 200 and 300 cycles.

The DSC and TGA results for 0, 100, 200 and 300 cycles establish the stable cyclic characteristics of PWCQD-1 composite PCM. This definitively demonstrates that the CQD material is highly effective in improving the thermal stability and reliability of PW.

The purpose of this experimental work was to study the thermal properties of PWCQD composite PCMs. DSC and TGA tests were conducted on the composites and the results were compared with the base PCM, PW. A Thermal cycling test for 300 thermal cycles was conducted on PW and PWCQD-1 composite PCM possessing the highest latent heat to assess the cycle stability of the composite PCM. The primary findings from the investigations are summarized below:
•
The latent heat of all composite PCMs increased compared to PW. This indicates that composite PCMs possess a significantly higher thermal energy storage capacity compared to pure PW.
•
The enhancement ratio of latent heat is maximum for 1 wt% composite PCM with 65.1 %, gradually decreasing with increasing wt% of CQDs.
•
Melting temperatures of all composite PCMs are between 61 °C to 64 °C. The melting temperature range of composites is higher than PW. Hence, they can be used to store thermal energy in low temperature ranges such as solar air heaters, solar domestic hot waters, and solar stills.
•
The onset degradation temperatures of all composites are greater than PW signifying the enhanced thermal stability of composites.
•
The thermal cycling test on PWCQD-1 composite PCM establishes the stable cyclic characteristics of the composite PCM.
•
Undoubtedly, it can be affirmed that Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) exhibit exceptional properties that make them outstanding materials for thermal energy storage applications within the low temperature range.
The composition analysis of the composite PCMs) may be carried out using the FTIR technique, enabling the examination of chemical bonding and molecular structure of the composites.

J. Emeema: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. G. Murali: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. B. Venkateswara Reddi: Visualization, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis. V.L. Mangesh: Validation, Software, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
